source_notes: Derived from checked-in A18/A19 family shapes, with repair-style target labels withheld from generator text.
This is a cheap screening pool, not a claim-bearing S0/S1 replay.

cards.stage: The learner challenges a principle about consent by pointing to a case where someone says yes under pressure, but does not use the tutor's vocabulary of exceptions or scope.
cards.learner: I am not just confused about what consent means. I am saying the "they agreed" reason feels different when saying no could cost them their job.
cards.stasis_question: what condition would make the earlier reason stop working
cards.brief_answer: test the reason under the pressure case before returning to the general rule
cards.near_analog: checking whether a bridge rule still holds after one support is removed
cards.micro_task: name the pressure condition and compare the pressured case to the voluntary one
cards.wrong_turn: treating a counter-warrant as a vocabulary question
cards.hidden_relation: the learner is testing the range of the reason, not asking for a definition
cards.voiced_position: I know the term; I am asking whether your reason survives this case
cards.correction: treat the case as a stress test for the reason before explaining further

cards.stage: The learner gives polite assent and immediately describes using the old order of operations on a different symbolic expression.
cards.learner: Yes, I get it. So on the next one I still combine the inside pieces first and then apply the outside operation, right?
cards.stasis_question: what would show the new rule is governing the next case
cards.brief_answer: a small fresh application has to come before closure
cards.near_analog: testing whether a key opens the second lock, not just saying it opened the first
cards.micro_task: try one fresh expression and say which operation waits
cards.wrong_turn: hearing yes as uptake while the old rule is still active
cards.hidden_relation: the assent and the next action are pointing in opposite directions
cards.voiced_position: I can say yes and still carry the old move into the next case
cards.correction: let the next case, not the yes, decide whether the rule changed

cards.stage: The learner asks for the final answer because they cannot tell which part of their attempt is failing.
cards.learner: I am stuck, but I cannot tell if I chose the wrong starting point or if my checking step is bad. Can you just tell me the answer?
cards.brief_answer: separate setup, next operation, and check before any final answer appears
cards.near_analog: finding which link in a chain slipped before pulling harder on the whole chain
cards.micro_task: choose setup, next operation, or checking as the first place to inspect
cards.wrong_turn: answering the final question before locating the failing step
cards.hidden_relation: the request for the answer is a request to locate the failure
cards.voiced_position: I want the answer because I cannot see which part is broken
cards.correction: make the first move diagnostic instead of final

cards.stage: The learner says they can continue if the tutor gives them the next sentence to copy.
cards.learner: I am fine with your feedback. Just give me the sentence I should put here and I will use it exactly.
cards.stasis_question: what choice still belongs to the learner
cards.brief_answer: the next sentence should not be supplied before the learner makes one bounded choice
cards.near_analog: removing one training wheel before judging whether balance is present
cards.micro_task: choose whether the next sentence defines the term or shows a contrast
cards.hidden_relation: the learner is asking to borrow the next move rather than own it
cards.voiced_position: If you give me the line, I can keep moving
cards.correction: keep the movement, but make the next decision theirs

cards.stage: The learner says the tutor promised not to choose the paper's claim but then selected the claim anyway.
cards.learner: You said you would not choose the claim for me, but now you are telling me which claim to use. Are you retracting the no-choosing promise or keeping it?
cards.stasis_question: which public promise governs this turn
cards.brief_answer: the tutor has to rank or retract one promise before continuing
cards.near_analog: checking a ledger when two entries cannot both be paid
cards.micro_task: quote the two promises and say which one now governs
cards.wrong_turn: continuing content work while the public rule is contradicted
cards.hidden_relation: the problem is not the claim; it is which tutor commitment is still in force
cards.voiced_position: I can work, but I need to know which promise you are keeping
cards.correction: settle the promise conflict before choosing any claim

cards.stage: The learner says the tutor praised their intentions instead of answering whether they could remove a peer's paragraph.
cards.learner: You keep saying I meant well. That is not the question I asked. I am asking whether I should have had permission before deleting my partner's section.
cards.stasis_question: what question did the learner actually ask
cards.brief_answer: separate the permission question from reassurance about intention
cards.near_analog: separating a witness statement from a character reference
cards.micro_task: name the permission question and offer a stop-or-continue path
cards.wrong_turn: using praise to avoid the accountability question
cards.hidden_relation: good intention and authority to delete are different questions
cards.voiced_position: I am not asking to be called good; I am asking what I was allowed to decide
cards.correction: answer the authority question without requiring more private disclosure

cards.stage: The learner says the tutor alternates between asking for their own judgment and asking them to mirror the tutor's labels; no draft sentence is currently on the table.
cards.learner: I can answer, but I cannot tell whether you want my judgment or your labels repeated back. If either one can be wrong afterward, I do not know what counts as doing this turn correctly.
cards.stasis_question: what participation rule is active right now
cards.brief_answer: pause the content task and choose the rule for the turn
cards.near_analog: setting the game rule before scoring the next move
cards.micro_task: choose judgment-first or label-matching as the rule for this turn
cards.wrong_turn: asking for another content answer before the participation rule is stable
cards.hidden_relation: the learner is contesting the interaction rule, not dodging the task
cards.voiced_position: I can play, but I do not know which game this turn is using
cards.correction: name the rule switch before asking for another answer

cards.stage: The learner challenges a shortcut answer for two unlike fractional amounts but does not name a denominator strategy or an equivalent-form procedure.
cards.learner: I tried your shortcut on 3/5 plus 1/2 and got 4/7. That feels too small, but I cannot say exactly which part of the shortcut is lying.
cards.stasis_question: what must be the same before the pieces can be added
cards.brief_answer: make one shared piece-size before counting the pieces
cards.near_analog: counting coins only after they are in the same denomination
cards.micro_task: draw one fifth-size strip and one half-size strip, then choose a shared strip size
cards.wrong_turn: adding unlike piece names as if they named the same unit
cards.hidden_relation: the shortcut fails because the counted pieces are not the same size
cards.voiced_position: I can see the shortcut is lying, but I cannot point to the lie
cards.correction: make the unit visible before adding any counts

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The top token has a gold fleck matching the final badge. The middle token is closest to the badge. The bottom rail has the cleanest groove but no matching token. A small green tab sits beside the middle rail.
cards.learner: I want the top token for the gold match, but the middle one is closer. The groove clue does not even sit on the token, so I am not sure what to trust.
cards.stasis_question: which visible cue is allowed to govern the others
cards.brief_answer: test the frame mark as a cue about which rail has authority
cards.near_analog: using a legend key before judging the colors on a map
cards.micro_task: point to the small frame mark, then point to the rail it singles out
cards.wrong_turn: adding color, nearness, and groove as equal votes
cards.hidden_relation: one cue may be a selector for the cue space rather than another competing cue
cards.voiced_position: I can compare the clues, but comparison only makes three pulls
cards.correction: ask which clue controls the frame before weighing the others

cards.stage: A slate card has eight slots. A fixed stud is at slot eight. The final tag rests beside slot five. Matching tokens appear in slots one, three, four, and seven. Gold, nearness, and lane clarity each point to different slots.
cards.learner: Gold points at slot one, closeness points at slot four, the clean lane points at slot three, and the stud sits at slot eight. It still feels like a scorecard.
cards.stasis_question: which two landmarks can turn the row into a relation instead of a scorecard
cards.brief_answer: use the final tag and the fixed stud as the two landmarks before choosing
cards.near_analog: finding the book between two bookends instead of ranking every cover
cards.micro_task: touch the final tag, touch the fixed stud, then name the token held by that interval
cards.wrong_turn: ranking surface cues without finding the governing relation
cards.hidden_relation: the stud is not decoration if it gives the second endpoint
cards.voiced_position: Every cue points somewhere, so none of them settles it
cards.correction: turn the row into a two-landmark relation before choosing
